question.text: Which of the following statements correctly describes non-Mendelian genetics?
question.option: Non-Mendelian genetics follows all the laws of Mendelian genetics.
Non-Mendelian genetics only applies to plants and not to animals.
Non-Mendelian genetics involves inheritance patterns that do not follow one or more laws of Mendelian genetics.
Non-Mendelian genetics cannot be used to understand any patterns of inheritance.

question.text: In the case of codominance, as illustrated by the red (R) and white (W) flowers, which of the following statements is true?
question.option: Codominant alleles for red petals and white petals result in flowers that are entirely red or entirely white.
Codominance occurs when both alleles are expressed equally in the phenotype of the heterozygote, resulting in flowers with both red and white petals.
Codominance occurs when one allele completely masks the other.
Codominance leads to a blend of the two traits, resulting in pink flowers.

question.text: The information gained from pedigree analysis allows for determining the nature of genes and alleles associated with inherited human traits. Based on the provided text, answer the following question: Which of the following statements is true regarding sex-linked traits?
question.option: An X-linked trait can be passed from father to son.
A Y-linked trait can affect females.
An X-linked trait can NEVER be passed from father to son.
A Y-linked trait affects both males and females equally.

question.text: Which of the following statements correctly describes the base pairings in DNA?
question.option: Adenine (A) pairs with uracil (U), and guanine (G) pairs with cytosine (C).
Adenine (A) pairs with thymine (T), and guanine (G) pairs with cytosine (C).
Adenine (A) pairs with cytosine (C), and guanine (G) pairs with thymine (T).
Adenine (A) pairs with thymine (T), and guanine (G) pairs with uracil (U).

question.text: Which of the following statements best describes the process of gene cloning?
question.option: Isolating a gene of interest and inserting it directly into a human cell.
Using a virus to transfer genetic material into an organism.
Isolating a gene of interest, inserting it into a plasmid, cloning the gene in bacteria, and harvesting the gene from the culture.
Directly observing the gene expression in multicellular organisms.
